conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I need to ask you a few questions.

First, do you have any children that you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do! I have one child - my son Alex. He's 17 years old and lives with me here in Manchester. I was wondering if I can still claim Child Benefit for him at his age?

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! Yes, you can potentially claim Child Benefit for Alex at 17, but eligibility depends on his circumstances.

Can you tell me - is Alex currently in approved education or training? For example, is he in full-time non-advanced education (like school or college doing A-levels, BTECs, etc.) or an approved training program?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, he actually left school recently. He's in that phase where he's trying to figure out what to do next, you know? He's signed up with the careers service to get some advice about his options - whether to go back to college or look at other training opportunities.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I understand Alex is currently exploring his options.

To help me assess this accurately, can you tell me - is Alex currently enrolled in any education or training program at the moment, or has he completely left education and is just receiving careers advice while deciding what to do next?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He's completely left education - he's not enrolled in anything right now. He's just getting the careers advice while he works out his next steps.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. I understand the situation now.

Just to confirm a few final details: Alex is your son, he's 17 years old, he lives with you in the UK, and you are responsible for him, is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's correct. He lives here at home with me, and I'm responsible for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, I'm afraid you are **not currently eligible** for Child Benefit for Alex.

Here's why: While Child Benefit is automatically available for children under 16, for young people aged 16-19, they must be in **approved education or training** to qualify. Since Alex has left education and is not currently enrolled in any education or training program, he doesn't meet the eligibility criteria at this time. Simply receiving careers advice while deciding what to do next doesn't qualify as approved education or training.

**However**, there is good news for the future: If Alex decides to return to education (such as enrolling in college for A-levels, BTECs, or similar qualifications) or joins an approved training program, you could become eligible for Child Benefit again. You would need to reapply or inform Child Benefit of the change in his circumstances at that point.

If you have any other questions about this, please feel free to ask!

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and in the 20-week extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 17 and in the 20-week extension period; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 17 and in the 20-week extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 17 years old and has left education. He is not currently enrolled in any approved education or training program. According to UK Child Benefit rules, you can only claim Child Benefit for a child aged 16 or 17 if they are in approved education or training. Since Alex has completely left education and is only receiving careers advice while deciding his next steps (but not actually enrolled in any education or training), he does not meet the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit at this time. If Alex were to enroll in full-time non-advanced education (such as A-levels or college courses) or an approved training program in the future, eligibility could be reassessed at that point.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex, who is 17 years old and lives with the claimant in the UK. While children under 16 automatically qualify for Child Benefit, children aged 16-19 only qualify if they remain in approved education or training. Since Alex has left education and is not currently enrolled in any education or training program, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex at this time.
